author: @objection_your_honor
text:
For indoor parts, I use PLA+ @220ºC. For outdoor parts, White PETG @230ºC. Both with the bed @65ºC.
ABS is yesterday's news and the micro-plastics it releases into the air while printing, not only stinks, it's carcinogenic.

author: @icebluscorpion
text: You can get even bigger stuff out of that little printer if you cut your model into pieces and add mortes and Tannen strategically before printing it after printing you will get a awesome big puzzle pieces that you can then snuggfit with glue together 😉trust me you won't regret it 😎. Also you may print in the wrong direction as long as you can make it stronger by inserting a threaded rod all the way through and bold plates on each end to get some slight compression. I recommend use rather multiple thin threaded rods than a massive one to reduce weight.

author: @seaniepie
text: Hi Robert. Thank you so much for all of these videos. I have a few quick maths question. If your gen is producing 55V, 200mW, how do we calc the equivalent power production as Wh? Or is it just 200mW/h? or 200mWh? And how does this relate to the motor ratings (obviously for motor, not generator) of 280V 20W? What are the generation characteristics when using this motor as a generator that cause it to only produce 200mW, or is it mainly down to the load, those LEDs you are using? Thanks again. Sean

replies.author: @davegoud
replies.text: Sean, you're correct, it's an underwhelming 200mW/h output.  The motor has either multiple coils and/or taps as there are multiple outputs: 280vdc, 15vdc, 6.5vac and 2 others that I have no idea.  But the main motor rating is 20w 280vcd @ 1300rpm.  The rpm is an important part of the equation.  If you turn the motor @ 1300rpm's you can achieve close to 20w/h.  As long as you maintain 1300rpm under load conditions.   Hope that answers your question

author: @DaremoKamen
text: Has anyone tried a horizontal axis wind turbine where the blades are Flettner rotors?  A vertical axis wind turbine at the tip of each rotor to spin it, then when they start to turn around the horizontal axis the apparent wind on the vertical axis turbines is greater causing each rotor to spin faster.  I'm sure an actual airfoil will be more efficient than an Flettner rotor, but possibly a trade off of simpler to make and/or stronger would make them more economical.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Aerodynamically, there will be less drag the way you ended up printing it.

Ideally would be to have a completely smooth featureless surface, naturally, but, if you can't get away from surface features, at least the best direction for those little ridges is to be slicing through the air, as opposed to dragging against the air.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Overlooking the surface layer texture direction and what kind of drag is caused. The bigger issue here is  centrifugal forces and how well are the 3d printed layers bonded together. It would be radially stronger if the layers were added circumfrentally rather than radially.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 That little thing ain't ever gunna spin fast enough to come apart.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542 Maybe so, but without fiberglass epoxy reinforcement, i wouldn't trust it to not fail before three month of blowing in the wind has passed let alone last 5 years as the plastic flexes and undergoes repeated plastic deformation. 

As for things being held by superglue and things flying apart, I posted one on my channel. I was lucky not to be hit by a flying magnet.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 While 1, I admit I have never even used a 3D printer, held a 3D printed part, or know the parameters of that specific print, my observations from other videos on 3D printed blades, combined with known factors in this situation [namely its reasonable peak RPM under load even in a strong gale wind], and the realistic mass of the part its self, tells me its at least 800 times stronger than it needs to be to do its job.

2, It has no magnets attached to it.

author: @FlyingPeteNZ
text: You could use vapour smoothing on the ABS parts, you put them in a closed container with some paper towel soaked in acetone for a period of time (need to test that but not too long), they mustn't actually touch the paper towels though (I use a metal baking rack).  This smooths the surface by remelting it which also probably achieves the same strengthening as the CA glue.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- also (experiment before using in critical components) I have used a quick dip in acetone or even a dip in acetone+ABS  ":slurry" it provides a rapid coating/smoothing without softening the (ESP if thin) underlying ABS (in a vapour chamber thin parts can warp), also  coating in clear epoxy provides some consolidation (helps in waterproofing / leak proofing parts...   so many ways to alter the output...

Heating prints with hot air gun or in an oven can help to consolidate layers, and close gaps, just use care..... Sadly I have had some very long print-time parts fail in post processing when "annealing"/normalising in ovens" - due to several mechanisms; differential / non isotropic shrinkage,  too hot of an oven temp- oops the sag....  and careful of z-axis growth at "periplastic" temps - make sure oven isn't too short.. lol...

author: @ste4bz
text: Awesome! Quick question.... if you had more blades would that improve your output? I know on full size turbines it's not done this way due to the cost of 4 blades not giving an increased output worth it for the cost of the extra blade but at small scale, maybe?? Or possibly second set of 3 stacked behind the first 3 of using the same 3d print? Looks like you might have enough room on your motor shaft.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Fewer blades tend to increase efficiency but require faster tip speed ratio. More blades tend to reduce efficency but they can provide more torque and don't need to spin as fast.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Power in a wind is determined by speed density and cross section. Not how many blades that sweep that cross section.  It should become apparent  why more blades introduce more drag.  And why additional blades give more torque on the axle, but at lower rotational speed.

replies.author: @Coxeysbodgering
replies.text: If you remember or look at the ceiling fan generator videos, Robs had 5 blades, the ones I sourced on had 3 blades and just sticking the multimeter to the plug pins it produced more voltage the 5

author: @goforbrokefilmstudios
text: You can make your prints as strong as injection molding by printing with 100% infill and then packing them in finely ground salt and heating them up to the melting point of the plastic and holding at that temperature for a while, and then letting it cool down slowly. CNC Kitchen did a nice video on this and included strength tests.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Weight  or flywheel weight has a limiting effect on turbine efficiency. In variable wind. 

To see why, try this thought experiment. Imagine the blades were 100× the weight they are now. It would take lots of time topspin them up and during that time, the tip speed ratio is less optimal and lower efficiency.  On the other side, let's say the wind slows down, and the wind turbine's massively heavy blades keep spinning the turbine which takes the energy stored in the  rotational momentum of the turbine and and dumps it back into the air acting like a big fan trying to speed up the air that's slowed down. 

Maximum energy is transferred at certain wind tip speed ratio five given turbine. Ideally a wind turbine blade would have zero weight to capture the maximum energy through even the smallest change in wind. But zero mass turbine is not possible. So we try to make them as light as we can with enough strength to handle the stresses of the wind and torque the generator's  axile. 

Rather than 100% infill, one might go the other direction and go with as little infill as possible. And use fiberglass and resin to provide strength.

replies.author: @jameshughes3014
replies.text: Wouldn't that cause warping? Probably not great for turbine blades.

replies.author: @goforbrokefilmstudios
replies.text: @jameshughes3014 No. The ground/powdered salt is packed in all around the part VERY tightly. If you watch the video by CNC Kitchen, you'll see how he does this.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @jameshughes3014 would it cause warping?  No. warmed sand or salt imobilzes the plastic so that it doesn't change shape. It's warmed up to just below the plastic's melting temperature. This helps create cross linking between layers over time without changing the shape. Then it is allowed to slowly cool so that no difference in in internal/external stresses are (for lack of a better word) baked into the plastic.

replies.author: @CottonTailJoe
replies.text: Yes very good method!

hi, if your 3d printing settings are right and are printing multiple higher things at the same time, try to put them closer together on the build plate. the "travel time" wil make a big drop wich can shave of a few hours in some prints. i have seen projects go from 18h to 14h this way. 
using a bigger nozzle can also help, not all engineering related projects need the fine detail.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Good tips. And thicker layer roughness can be smoothed with time in an acetone vapor chamber.  That might also help the plastic polimere layers better cross link and better bond.

author: @adymode
text: Alas the motor doesnt seem well spec'ed for this kind of turbine. It is spec'ed to draw or deliver 0.1 amp 'into' 280v when spun at 1300 rev/minute. It 'pumps' less amps at lower speeds and more at higher speeds but risks burn out.  When assessing motors as generators , the amperage is crucial as you cant draw much more amps than it is designed for regardless of what voltage it is wished to draw from the generator (or let current flow out) When you let current flow from the generator, its slows down . On the other hand if you dont slow the blades down at the right rpm, they spin to fast, shed captured wind energy as vibration and noise. Its a fine balancing act... eg. The printed turbine blades are large enough to capture about 30 watts from moderate wind - if you take 0.1 amps out into a battery at around 15volts, that will only be about 1.5 watts taken out of the blades energy, the remaining 20+watts will keep accelerating the blades until shaking and noise arrests them - can get fun :)

replies.author: @3dpchiron709
replies.text: Perhaps gearing or pulleys would help with this, or something like a cvt?

replies.author: @adymode
replies.text: Gearing is often used in the megawatt scale windturbines, because the generators dont need as strong magnetic fields when working at higher revs. But other megawatt scale windturbines avoid gearing and just beef up the generator, because "direct drive" has much fewer parts to include and maintain. A famous engineer is quoted - "the best part is no part". Safe speed for the printed prop might be around 500 rpm (?) similar sized rc helicopter blades do around that but they are lighter. In the test we can estimate it was turning at about 250 rpm as the voltage raises linear with the rotors frequency. The power output is likely to scale with the square of the frequency. Gearing could help it generate more at desirable speeds, but the trickiest thing with these is it only takes a stronger gust of wind to send everything into overdrive, so really big margins are required to end up with something practical. I tried a couple of times to make one, got around 10 watts on a breezy day with rc helicopter parts, but the speed was such a hazard, I put it down to experience and went for solar kit instead. It wonderfully tranquil in comparison : )

author: @12thsonofisrael

And, the a/c fan comes with some built-in weather resistance.

I wonder if magnets simply placed on the outside of the fan motor would increase the power output without causing to much distortion? 🤔

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: As a brushless dc motor (BLDC),  the motor has magnets on the rotor with coils on the stator.  Adding stationary magnets is not going to help a BLDC motor generator.  At best theybwond do anything to help. At worst they will encourage saturation of iron core (if used) for one pole and not another. When a magnetic core is saturated, it will cause flux leakage . In other words the core can not contain all the magnetic field and some of the rotating magnetic field will not pass inside the coils that surround the magnetic core.  Thus external  magnets could hurt the performance.
